posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
In haste, here are some more photos of the grapes in Burgundy this year that may be of interest.

First, a tour of the Romanée-Saint-Vivant… you’ll not different canopy management strategies, the different fruit that derives from different rootstock & Pinot Noir selections, different farming (especially the effects of no till at Arnoux Lachaux), different yields…


Domaine de la Romanée-Conti Romanée-Saint-Vivant 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Domaine d’Arlot Romanée-Saint-Vivant 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Arnoux-Lachaux Romanée-Saint-Vivant 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Cathiard Romanée-Saint-Vivant 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Dujac Romanée-Saint-Vivant 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Louis Latour Romanée-Saint-Vivant 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Leroy Romanée-Saint-Vivant 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Leroy Romanée-Saint-Vivant grapes by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

link_counts.title: Domaine de la Romanée-Conti Romanée-Saint-Vivant 2020 | Flickr

posts.cooked:

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Here is Roumier’s Amoureuses:


Roumier Amoureuses 2020 (1) by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Roumier Amoureuses 2020 (2) by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

…and here is Mugnier’s:


Mugnier Amoureuses 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

Here is Coche’s Perrières:


Coche-Dury Perrières 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

…and here is d’Auvenay’s Gouttes d’Or:


d’Auvenay Gouttes d’Or 2020 (1) by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


d’Auvenay Gouttes d’Or 2020 (2) by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

…and for now, that’s about all I have time for. Happy to try to respond to any questions that may arise!

posts.name: Bill Sweeney
posts.cooked: Great pics! Thanks.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked: William - thank you for these, they’re great!

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: Interesting the RC vines look remarkably similar to Dujac. Those along with Leroy seem quite manicured and healthy. The fruit zones are spaced out evenly. Obviously great viticulture.

posts.name: Nick Christie
D Borczon
posts.cooked: And why I have questions about Latour. Don’t own a single bottle.

posts.name: SeanSydney
posts.cooked:
Fantastic pictures. The D’Arlot vines look like they’re about to collapse from the numerous giant clusters - yields seem so high.

Latour…did the majority of their fruit even ripen? What is going on there?

Arnoux, yikes. I know in the other thread you said that no-till is proving to be a difficult choice this year, and those grapes look, to be honest, a bit raisinated and sickly. I see what they were going for - nice small berries with plenty of airspace - but it seems like they overshot. Do you think the lack of tilling has anything to do with it?

By purely visual inspection to my eyes, it looks like Leroy, Dugat-Py, and Coche are the winners with Bachelet close behind. The DRC & Dujac fruit looks incredibly healthy, too.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: Well, darned if I can tell they are different root-stocks!

posts.name: M. Ch@o
posts.cooked: The Dugat Py gevrey looks great

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked:
Interesting the RC vines look remarkably similar to Dujac. Those along with Leroy seem quite manicured and healthy. The fruit zones are spaced out evenly. Obviously great viticulture.

Was going to say the same. DRC, Dujac, and Leroy grapes look beautiful. Almost uniform in color and size.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Fantastic pictures. The D’Arlot vines look like they’re about to collapse from the numerous giant clusters - yields seem so high.

Latour…did the majority of their fruit even ripen? What is going on there?

Arnoux, yikes. I know in the other thread you said that no-till is proving to be a difficult choice this year, and those grapes look, to be honest, a bit raisinated and sickly. I see what they were going for - nice small berries with plenty of airspace - but it seems like they overshot. Do you think the lack of tilling has anything to do with it?

By purely visual inspection to my eyes, it looks like Leroy, Dugat-Py, and Coche are the winners with Bachelet close behind. The DRC & Dujac fruit looks incredibly healthy, too.

I don’t think Latour have dropped the second crop, unlike the other producers in Romanée-Saint-Vivant in the photo! So that’s why you are seeing green clusters. Dropping the second crop does make a meaningful difference, so it might be something for them to consider in the future…

And yes, clearly the Arnoux vines suffered a lot with all the competition from the grasses. I wonder if mowing might have helped? I discussed that briefly with Charles and he said mowing favored undesirable plant species so he planned to roll. These grapes have already been picked, in any case. I guess the yields were below 15 hl/ha?

In summation, I think Leroy had a really good year this year, albeit with heavy and regular spraying somewhat later in the season that one might have wished for (the smell of sulfur in the vines was quite pungent). Dugat-Py at this stage have among the best, if not the best, farming in the Côte d’Or: obviously the winemaking was a bit pumped up in the past, but when you look at the grapes you understand why the wines are somewhat inky and concentrated; with Loïc’s lighter touch in the cellar, that fruit now gets the winemaking it deserves, and even if it’s an uphill struggle I’ll continue to encourage people to put preconceptions aside and try the recent vintages. Dujac and DRC appear to have done very well, too - DRC had dropped the sunburnt clusters before these photos were taken, which will help come harvest. I guess another observation would be how high are the yields Roumier and Cathiard hang, while nonetheless making superb wines. Every time you hear a wine merchant or wine writer talk about how low the yields are at those addresses, think of these photos! Of course, even these rather generous crops are nowhere near what might have been achieved twenty years ago when Burgundy was at its most fecund (and fertilized).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Since I have a bit of time on my hands this evening, here is a tour around Bonnes-Mares. I suspect all this fruit will be picked this week…


Mugnier Bonnes-Mares 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Roumier Bonnes-Mares 2020 (1) by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Roumier Bonnes-Mares 2020 (2) by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


d’Auvenay Bonnes-Mares 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Bertheau Bonnes-Mares 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

posts.name: SeanSydney
posts.cooked:
Since I have a bit of time on my hands this evening, here is a tour around Bonnes-Mares. I suspect all this fruit will be picked this week…

Would love, in another lifetime where I’m reincarnated as a far wealthier man, to do a Roumier/Mugnier comparison of this and the Amoureuses. Like you said, the generous yields of the former and the relative paucity of fruit of the latter are striking (though I’m sure both will be brilliant).

posts.name: rob klafter
posts.cooked: while we are drilling down. Can you comment on the red vs white soil of Bonnes Mares? In terms of what we are seeing? My understanding is Roumier is mainly White. Do we see red? and while we have a deep talk, can you comment on the profile imparted by red soil vs white?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
One last post this evening… Soon you will start hearing about “the low yielding 2020 vintage”, and certainly some producers do have very low yields. However, this is far from uniformly the case, as these two photographs testify:


High yeilding Pinot Noir in 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Copious crop in Meursault Rougeots in 2020 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

link_counts.title: Copious crop in Meursault Rougeots in 2020 | WilliamGFKelley | Flickr
High yeilding Pinot Noir in 2020 | WilliamGFKelley | Flickr

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
while we are drilling down. Can you comment on the red vs white soil of Bonnes Mares? In terms of what we are seeing? My understanding is Roumier is mainly White. Do we see red? and while we have a deep talk, can you comment on the profile imparted by red soil vs white?

Roumier has a pretty even mix of both these days (this has evolved over the years), and there is a photo above from each of his two most important parcels… I do think the white soils with higher percentages of active limestone seem to make a more delicate, fragrant wine (d’Auvenay being the archetype thereof), but honestly, when you see the differences in viticultural practices within what is a very small area at the end of the day, it would be naive to start casually attributing differences in wine character and quality to soil types. Not that soil types don’t matter, but one can only isolate them when all things are equal—and they manifestly aren’t!

posts.cooked: William, what are the ripeness levels this year? It seems to be a very early harvest. Are we looking at another 2003 here?

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
William, what are the ripeness levels this year? It seems to be a very early harvest. Are we looking at another 2003 here?

Hope not. I keep an eye on Beaune temps through the summer, and I can’t remember anything like the extended extreme heat waves of 2003. Also curious about the vintage. I assume it will be another in a string of “solar” vintages, exactly how is the question.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: Damn, some of those Roumier Amoureuses shots are almost pornographic, the bunches look so abundant, perfect, and beautifully colored. I’ll never be able to afford a bottle of that stuff but at the standard 400 grapes/bottle I’d be willing to pay $5-10 to eat one of those grapes!

Love your pictures and insight!

It’s nice not to have to choose, but if I had to pick two desert island bottles they might be 1999 Domaine d’Auvenay Chevalier-Montrachet and 1919 Clos des Lambrays.

Hope not. I keep an eye on Beaune temps through the summer, and I can’t remember anything like the extended extreme heat waves of 2003. Also curious about the vintage. I assume it will be another in a string of “solar” vintages, exactly how is the question.

No, I don’t think it will be too like 2003 in the end! Things have cooled off nicely now… Lots of Côte de Nuits domaines will harvest this week. But of course, only time will tell how the wines will be…

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
Roumier used to have as much red as white soil . From 2016 (I think), he has added white soil.
Red gives more structured solid wine, white soil leads to more refined fragrant earlier maturing wine. Expect more “Chambollic” (as opposed to Morey) Bonnes Mares from now on… if you can afford them as prices are going up big time.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Since I have a bit of time on my hands this evening, here is a tour around Bonnes-Mares. I suspect all this fruit will be picked this week…

Would love, in another lifetime where I’m reincarnated as a far wealthier man, to do a Roumier/Mugnier comparison of this and the Amoureuses. Like you said, the generous yields of the former and the relative paucity of fruit of the latter are striking (though I’m sure both will be brilliant).

A number of us did a Mugnier vs Roumier dinner recently of Fuees vs. Le Cras. Let’s just say they were not hard to tell apart.

Yes, I realize they’re different crus, but the winemaker signatures are pretty distinct. There was a Barthod ringer, and we all figured it was a Roumier, lol.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
William, thank you for doing these threads. I’m much newer to being serious about wine relative to most people on this forum, and the viticultural aspect of winemaking is the area about which I know least. These threads have been super interesting and informative.

Even for people who have been drinking Burgundy for over 40 years think these threads have been super interesting and informative. I have never read things quite like these.
